subspecies: J. h. oreganus (Oregon group -- Pacific coast, the most common Dark-eyed Junco in the PNW)
season: resident (year-round in forest edges, suburban yards, trailhead margins, and second-growth clearings)

coordinates.description: Beacon Hill / Puget Sound reference; the Oregon form ranges from southern Alaska through the Pacific coast to Baja California

plumage.overall: Dark slate-to-black hood sharply contrasting with warm rusty-brown back and pink-brown flanks
plumage.hood: Dark slate to black (males), paler gray (females)
plumage.tail: White outer tail feathers -- flash conspicuously in flight (visual signal)
plumage.bill: Pale pinkish -- diagnostic junco field mark
plumage.field_id: Dark hood + rusty back + pink flanks + white outer tail flash + pink bill. Instantly distinguishable from Slate-colored form.

taxonomy_note.status: CONTESTED -- formerly a separate species, lumped into Dark-eyed Junco complex by AOU in 1973, may be split again based on molecular phylogenetics
key_studies: Milá et al. 2007 -- significant genetic divergence between forms
Friis et al. 2016 -- narrow, stable hybrid zone in Great Plains
taxonomy_note.lesson: Taxonomy is a human classification applied to biological continua. The Oregon Junco's history is the history of where to draw the line on a gradient.

frequency_range.song_trill_hz: 3000-6000 (single pitch per song, maintained throughout trill)
frequency_range.register: Mid-range (3-6 kHz -- comfortably within human hearing range)

trill_metrics.fusion_threshold: ~15 notes/s (above this rate, discrete pulses perceived as continuous tone)
trill_metrics.perceived_quality: Metallic buzz / sustained tone (despite being structurally discrete)
trill_metrics.structural_reality: Discrete individual note pulses with brief gaps between them
trill_metrics.perceptual_result: Continuous-sounding metallic tone above fusion threshold
trill_metrics.key_principle: The analog-to-digital boundary -- discrete pulses perceived as continuous signal when repetition rate exceeds temporal resolution

chip_note_metrics.comparison_to_bushtit: Louder, sharper, more individually distinctive than the bushtit's noise-floor seep

theory_nodes.concept: Trill as tremolo (rapid repetition creating illusion of sustained tone)
examples: Violin tremolo: rapid bow alternation on one note -- discrete strokes perceived as continuous tone
Mandolin tremolo picking: rapid plectrum strokes creating sustain on a plucked instrument
Piano tremolo: rapid key re-strikes creating sustain on a percussion instrument
Timpani roll: rapid mallet strikes creating the illusion of a sustained drum tone
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The tremolo is the musical exploitation of the flicker fusion threshold. The junco's trill is the biological original. Discrete attacks perceived as continuous tone -- the same principle in biology, music, radio, film, and LED technology.

theory_nodes.concept: Stroboscopic effect / flicker fusion in acoustics (analog-to-digital boundary)
examples: Strobe light: discrete flashes perceived as continuous illumination above fusion frequency
Film projection: 24 fps perceived as continuous motion
LED PWM dimming: on/off flashing perceived as steady light at set brightness
AM radio: carrier wave on/off switching perceived as audio tone above modulation frequency
Junco trill: discrete note pulses perceived as continuous buzzy tone above ~15 notes/s
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem demonstrated in biology. Discrete samples (trill notes) reconstructed as continuous signal (perceived tone) when sampling rate (trill rate) exceeds the auditory system's temporal resolution threshold.

theory_nodes.concept: Metallic timbre (rich harmonic spectrum -- strong upper partials)
examples: Harpsichord vs. piano: harpsichord has more upper harmonic content = 'metallic' quality
Steel-string vs. nylon-string guitar: steel is brighter, more harmonically rich
Snare drum (snares on) vs. tom-tom: snare wires add high-frequency metallic buzz
Trumpet vs. French horn: trumpet is brighter, more forward, more 'metallic'
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: 'Metallic' in acoustic terms means rich in upper harmonics -- the fundamental frequency accompanied by strong overtone content extending well above the fundamental, giving the sound a hard, bright, ringing edge. The junco's metallic quality places it at the bright, harmonically rich end of the avian timbral spectrum.

theory_nodes.concept: Ostinato at maximum rate (repetition becoming frequency)
examples: Baroque ground bass (chaconne, passacaglia): repeated bass figure underpinning variation
Steve Reich phasing: repeated patterns shifting against each other until they recombine
Philip Glass arpeggios: rapid repetitive figures creating a continuous texture
Junco trill: one note repeated so fast the figure dissolves into a tone -- the ostinato's endpoint
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The junco's trill is an ostinato reduced to its absolute minimum: one note at maximum rate. At this rate, the pattern (repetition) disappears and only the product (tone) remains. The figure dissolves into its own frequency. This is the limit case of repetition -- the point where time-domain pattern becomes frequency-domain signal.

theory_nodes.concept: Edge ecology as musical threshold (cadence ornament at the ecotone)
examples: Classical trill ornament at cadence point: the note AT the boundary between phrases
Baroque cadential trill: ornaments the penultimate note before resolution
Tremolo as mood transition in orchestral writing: signals shift between dynamic or tonal sections
Junco at forest edge: the bird AT the boundary between forest interior and open ground
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The trill ornaments the boundary. In music, the trill decorates the cadence point -- the note at the threshold between tension and resolution. In ecology, the junco trills at the ecotone -- the threshold between forest and field. The trill IS the sound of transition.

examples: AOU 1973 lump: five species merged into one based on hybridization (Biological Species Concept)
Molecular phylogenetics (2007, 2016): significant genetic divergence suggests they may be split
The hybrid zone is narrow and stable, not expanding -- limited gene flow
The bird doesn't change. The label does.
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Where do you draw the line between genres? Jazz and blues? Classical and romantic? The boundary is a human decision applied to a continuum. The junco complex is the taxonomic equivalent of the genre boundary problem: five forms on a gradient, with the line drawn differently depending on which criterion you prioritize.

cross_references.title: Varied Thrush (Degree 2)
cross_references.connection: CONTINUOUS VS. DISCRETE. The deepest connection in the circle. Both produce sounds perceived as sustained tones at a single pitch. But the thrush's tone is genuinely continuous (analog -- solid spectrogram line). The junco's trill is structurally discrete (digital -- row of individual dashes). Same perceptual output, opposite structural architecture. The thrush HOLDS a note. The junco BUILDS a note from atoms.

cross_references.title: Built & Physical Systems
cross_references.connection: NYQUIST / SAMPLING. The junco trill demonstrates the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem in biology. Discrete samples (trill notes) are reconstructed as continuous signal (perceived tone) when the sampling rate (trill rate) exceeds the auditory system's Nyquist threshold (flicker fusion frequency). Shannon's theorem in feathers.

cross_references.title: LED Research
cross_references.connection: PERSISTENCE OF VISION. PWM dimming, frame rate, flicker fusion -- the junco trill is the acoustic version of every visual persistence-of-vision technology. Discrete on/off pulses above the fusion threshold are perceived as continuity. Light, sound, film, biology -- the same principle across domains.

cross_references.title: Moody Blues Research
cross_references.connection: THRESHOLD ARCHITECTURE. The junco lives at the ecological threshold (forest edge). MDB documents musical threshold architecture (boundaries between sections, genres, movements). The trill ornaments the cadence point. The junco ornaments the ecotone. Edge as structural position in both domains.

cross_references.title: Living Systems of the PNW
cross_references.connection: Edge ecology, ecotone dynamics, forest-meadow mosaic, the edge effect (increased productivity and species diversity at habitat boundaries). Vertical partitioning of the ecotone with bushtit (degree 10): bushtit in shrub canopy, junco on the ground.

cross_references.title: Seattle 360 Music
cross_references.connection: Part A parallel: Joe Brazil at degree 11. Both are community-embedded, edge-dwelling figures -- always present at the threshold. Brazil was the neighborhood musician between venues, between genres, between stage and street. The junco is the neighborhood bird between forest and yard, between wild and domestic. Fixtures of place.

cross_references.title: Bushtit (Degree 10)
cross_references.connection: EDGE PARTITIONING. Both are edge/ecotone species at the forest margin. The bushtit works the shrub canopy; the junco works the ground layer. Vertical partitioning of the same edge habitat. Both are residents, year-round, common in suburban Seattle. The bushtit's signal is at the noise floor (energy 1); the junco's trill is above it (energy 2).

cross_references.title: Pacific Wren (Degree 1)
cross_references.connection: TRILL COMPARISON. The wren's cascade contains trills as one element within a complex song of 100+ notes. The junco's entire song IS a trill -- nothing else. The wren embeds the trill in maximum complexity. The junco isolates it in minimum context. Maximum vs. minimum framing for the same acoustic technique.

cross_references.title: Red-breasted Nuthatch (Degree 5)
cross_references.connection: REPETITION RATE. Both repeat a single note. The nuthatch repeats at metronomic rate below the fusion threshold -- you hear individual beats (click, click, click). The junco repeats above the fusion threshold -- you hear a continuous buzz. Same strategy (single-note repetition), different rate, qualitatively different perception. The nuthatch is the metronome. The junco is the tremolo.

interspecies_network.status: NOT EXTENDED -- the Oregon Junco does not participate in the chickadee alarm broadcasting network as a broadcaster or documented eavesdropper. It benefits passively from alarm behavior in mixed-species flocks.
interspecies_network.junco_role: The Oregon Junco forages on the ground at forest edges, often in loose proximity to mixed-species flocks containing chickadees and other passerines. It benefits from general alarm behavior but is not a documented participant in the A-B-C-D alarm protocol. Its own communication operates on a separate channel: song trill (territory/mate) and chip note (contact/alarm). The junco introduces a THIRD communication modality to the forest: the trill as territorial/mating signal.
interspecies_network.note: The alarm network remains at 4 nodes (2 broadcasters, 2 receivers). The swarm cohesion network (bushtit) remains at 1 node. The junco introduces a trill-based territorial signal that operates alongside both existing networks -- the forest now has three parallel communication channels.

part_a_parallel.shared_principle: Community-embedded, edge-dwelling, always present. Joe Brazil was the neighborhood musician -- always at the threshold between venues, between genres, between stage and street. The Oregon Junco is the neighborhood bird -- always at the threshold between forest and yard, between wild and domestic. Both are fixtures of place. Both define the edge they inhabit.
resonance_points: Edge-dwelling: Brazil worked the boundary between the jazz club and the neighborhood. The junco works the boundary between the forest and the yard. Both are threshold figures -- not deep inside, not fully outside, but AT the edge where two worlds meet.

Community-embedded: Brazil was not a touring star but a community fixture -- the musician who was always there, always playing, always part of the local fabric. The junco is the same -- the most common backyard bird in Seattle, always present at the feeder, always trilling from the low branch at the forest margin.

Persistent presence: Both define their locale through consistent, repeated presence. Brazil played the same venues year after year. The junco trills the same trill from the same edge perch year-round. Resident. Reliable. The sound of the place.

Threshold signal: Brazil's music carried between the inside (the club) and the outside (the street). The junco's trill carries between the inside (the forest) and the outside (the clearing). Both signals cross the boundary in both directions.

degree_arc.position: The metallic trill -- discrete pulses perceived as continuous tone. The analog-to-digital boundary in biological acoustics. The tremolo in feathers.
degree_arc.progression: Silence (0) -> cascade complexity (1) -> sustained tone (2) -> referential syntax (3) -> rhythmic motif (4) -> steady beat (5) -> upslurred question (6) -> harmonic series melody (7) -> ultrasonic threshold (8) -> variation on a theme (9) -> minimum viable signal / swarm (10) -> metallic trill / discrete-to-continuous (11)
degree_arc.key_insight: Degree 11 introduces three new dimensions: (1) THE TRILL -- rapid single-note repetition above the temporal fusion threshold, creating the perception of a continuous tone from discrete pulses; this is the analog-to-digital boundary in biological acoustics, the biological tremolo, the Nyquist threshold demonstrated in song. (2) CONTESTED TAXONOMY -- the Oregon Junco's lumping/splitting history makes classification itself a subject of the research, not just a background fact. (3) EDGE ECOLOGY AS THRESHOLD -- the junco's forest-edge habitat maps to the musical cadence point, the trill to the cadential ornament, the ecotone to the transition between sections.

synthesis_types.degree_4: Rhythmic cell (motif -- 4 notes with internal temporal structure)
synthesis_types.degree_5: Metronomic pulse (single note, steady rate -- pure temporal reference)
synthesis_types.degree_6: Pitch sweep (continuous ascending frequency glide -- directional change)
synthesis_types.degree_7: Harmonic series navigation (pitch selection from overtone ratios -- mathematical melody)
synthesis_types.degree_8: Ultra-high ascending series (discrete ascending notes at the audiometric boundary -- threshold crossing)
synthesis_types.degree_9: Variation on combinatorial syntax (same A-B-C-D structure, modified tempo/pitch/timbre -- the cover version)
synthesis_types.degree_10: Minimum viable signal (single-type contact call at noise floor -- swarm cohesion from 1-bit presence)
synthesis_types.degree_11: Tremolo / trill (single-note rapid repetition above fusion threshold -- discrete pulses perceived as continuous tone)

register_progression.degree_5: Mid-range (1.5-3.5 kHz fundamental, harmonics to 4+ kHz)
register_progression.degree_7: Mid-range to treble (1.5-4.0 kHz fundamental, overtones higher)
register_progression.degree_8: Ultra-high (6-10 kHz -- above audiometric ceiling for many adults)
register_progression.degree_9: Alto-soprano (2.5-5.5+ kHz -- shifted higher than degree 3, same register zone)
register_progression.degree_10: Ultra-high / noise floor (6-9 kHz -- in the same ultra-high register as degree 8 but quieter and structurally simpler)
register_progression.degree_11: Mid-range (3-6 kHz -- comfortably within human hearing, the first return to accessible mid-range since degree 5)

content_progression.degree_1: Identity + fitness (who is singing, how well)
content_progression.degree_3: Referential threat information (what is there, how dangerous)
content_progression.degree_4: Territorial identity through rhythm (I am here, this is my pattern)
content_progression.degree_5: Temporal grid (the steady beat that locates all other rhythms in time)
content_progression.degree_6: Identity through voice alone (the call IS the species)
content_progression.degree_7: Mathematical consonance (pitch selection follows the harmonic series)
content_progression.degree_8: Conspecific high-canopy signal (private channel at top of frequency band)
content_progression.degree_9: Referential threat information (SAME as degree 3 -- the content is conserved; the delivery varies)
content_progression.degree_10: Pure presence / flock cohesion (I am here -- no predator encoding, no territory, no mate attraction. 1 bit.)
content_progression.degree_11: Territory + mate attraction through trill (I am here, I am fit, this is my edge -- territorial song from the forest margin)

ecosystem_progression.degree_9: Coastal conifer (wet maritime -- Sitka spruce, western hemlock, western redcedar)
ecosystem_progression.degree_10: Shrubby woodland edge (ecotone -- the transition zone between forest and open habitat)
ecosystem_progression.degree_11: Forest edge (ecotone -- the threshold between forest interior and open ground)

ecology.habitat_requirement: Forest edges, second-growth margins, suburban yards with adjacent tree cover, trailhead clearings, logged edges with residual canopy. Requires the boundary between forest and open ground.
ecology.niche: Forest edge specialist -- the ecotone. Ground-foraging at the boundary between cover and exposure. Scratches in leaf litter, hops on trails, feeds at the interface.
ecology.foraging_strategy: Ground gleaner -- scratches in leaf litter with both feet simultaneously (double-scratch technique), exposing seeds, insects, and other invertebrates. Readily comes to bird feeders.
ecology.diet: Seeds (especially in winter -- millet, sunflower, weed seeds), insects (especially in breeding season -- beetles, caterpillars, ants, flies), spiders, and other small invertebrates.
ecology.nesting: Nest on the ground or in low vegetation at forest edges. Cup nest of grass, bark strips, and moss, lined with fine grass, hair, and feathers. Typically hidden under overhanging vegetation or in a bank cut.
ecology.white_tail_flash: The diagnostic white outer tail feathers flash conspicuously when the bird takes flight. This visual signal may serve multiple functions: startling predators, communicating flock alarm, and species recognition.

conservation.trend: Stable. One of the most abundant birds in North America.
conservation.threats: Domestic cat predation (ground foraging = maximum cat-hunting zone), window strikes (readily approaches structures), pesticide exposure from lawn chemicals, habitat loss where forest edges are paved over
conservation.vulnerability: Highly resilient -- thrives in edge habitat, suburban landscapes, and disturbed areas that human land use creates and expands. One of the most common feeder birds in the PNW.
conservation.unique_feature: The Oregon form's distinctiveness from other Dark-eyed Junco forms, combined with its contested taxonomy, makes it a case study in how classification decisions affect conservation priority -- subspecies receive less protection than full species under most frameworks.

key_sites.name: Seattle suburban neighborhoods
key_sites.description: The most common winter feeder bird in Seattle. Any yard with adjacent trees or shrubs will have Oregon Juncos from October through March, and resident pairs year-round.

key_sites.name: Discovery Park forest edges
key_sites.description: Forest-meadow ecotone along trails and at the transition between old-growth remnants and open bluffs.

key_sites.name: Seward Park perimeter
key_sites.description: Forest edge along the shore loop trail, where mature forest meets maintained lawn and lakeside habitat.

key_sites.name: Trailheads throughout Puget Sound lowlands
key_sites.description: Juncos are the bird hopping on the gravel at parking lots, picnic areas, and trailhead margins throughout the region.

spectrogram_reference.status: ADDRESSED -- Macaulay Library provides recordings showing the discrete-pulse structure of the trill at constant pitch, and the sharp chip note
spectrogram_reference.note: Spectrographic analysis of the Oregon Junco trill shows a row of individual note dashes at constant pitch -- a DOTTED line on the spectrogram, contrasting with the Varied Thrush's SOLID line (degree 2). This visual difference IS the structural difference: discrete vs. continuous. The chip note appears as a short, broad-band vertical mark -- a click.
